fullTitle: MITRE ATT&CK for Industrial Control Systems
description: MITRE ATT&CK for ICS is a knowledge base of adversary tactics and techniques specifically targeting Industrial Control Systems. Unlike the Enterprise ATT&CK matrix, ICS ATT&CK covers techniques that manipulate, disrupt, or destroy industrial processes rather than just IT systems. It is derived from real-world incidents including Stuxnet, Industroyer/Crashoverride, TRITON/TRISIS, and Sandworm operations.
useInThisRepo: Each ATT&CK for ICS technique referenced in IEC 62443 SRs and control library shows which techniques the control mitigates. The Threats view allows navigating from a known attack technique to the controls that defend against it — the 'red team lens' of the repository.

tactics.name: Initial Access
tactics.description: Techniques to gain initial foothold in an OT environment

tactics.name: Execution
tactics.description: Techniques to run adversary-controlled code on OT systems

tactics.name: Persistence
tactics.description: Techniques to maintain access to OT systems across restarts

tactics.name: Privilege Escalation
tactics.description: Techniques to gain higher-level permissions in OT systems

tactics.name: Evasion
tactics.description: Techniques to avoid detection in OT environments

tactics.name: Discovery
tactics.description: Techniques to learn about OT environment topology and assets

tactics.name: Lateral Movement
tactics.description: Techniques to move from initial access point into OT environment

tactics.name: Collection
tactics.description: Techniques to gather OT process data and configurations

tactics.name: Command and Control
tactics.description: Techniques to communicate with compromised OT systems

tactics.name: Inhibit Response Function
tactics.description: Techniques to prevent safety and protection functions from responding to failures

tactics.name: Impair Process Control
tactics.description: Techniques to manipulate, disable, or damage physical processes

tactics.name: Impact
tactics.description: Techniques to disrupt, damage, or destroy OT systems and physical processes
